<argument>
The title points to Earth’s ultimate social “gift,” not merely its economic tool. In the story, Earth uses trade, credit, and corporations to take over Zur—but at the end Broderick explains that this commercial system was only the method. The real goal was to dismantle Zur’s oppressive hierarchy, where rank and violence governed family and society.

Broderick states the end plainly: <quote>“you'll be working for us ... until the children of Earth and Zur are equal in knowledge and therefore equal partners. That's why we had to break down your caste system.”</quote> That is the clearest statement of what Earth is giving Zur: equality in place of caste.

The story then confirms the social change in Zotul’s own life. He immediately realizes: <quote>“And that is why my brothers did not beat me when I failed!”</quote> Earlier, beatings enforced his inferior status; by the end, that structure has eroded. So “capitalism” is only the instrument Earth deploys. The actual “gift from Earth” is the destruction of the caste system.
</argument>
